content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
C H A R MAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CoMMissioN
December 13,
1995
Mr.
Michael F.
Byrne
Michael Byrne
& Affiliates
Governmental Relations
980 Ninth Street
Suite 1600
Sacramento, California
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A’-95—384
Dear Mr.
Byrne:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
“revolving door” provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”) •1
QUESTION
May you work for a nonprofit organization which received a
grant from the Department of Conservation during your tenure as
Director, if the work is unrelated to the Department of
Conservation or its grant program?
CONCLUS ION
The Act’s revolving door provisions would not prohibit you
from working for a nonprofit organization which received a grant
from the Department of Conservation while you were Director,
as
long as
(a)
you do not represent the organization for compensation
before the Department of Conservation,
and
(b)
you do not
undertake work in any way related to the grant or any other
contract or proceeding in which the Department of Conservation and
the organization were involved while you were Director.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18995.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—384
Page
2
FACTS
As stated in your initial request for advice,
you were
Director of the Department of Conservation from 1991 to 1995.
You
resigned your position on October 15,
1995 and opened a
governmental relations firm,
Michael Byrne and Affiliates
(“MB&A”).
MB&A represents the interests of private businesses,
institutions, associations, governmental entities and individuals
before the state and federal government.
Among the services
MB&A
provides are issues management and advocacy, permit approvals,
sales of products and services to government entities,
and public
affairs spokesperson.
As Director of the Department of Conservation,
you
administered the beverage container recycling program as well as
concerns related to natural resources conservation, mining,
and
waste diversion.
You also conducted interagency relations with
the Trade and Commerce Agency, the Office of Planning and
Research, the Office of Emergency Services, the Seismic Safety
Commission,
and the Departments of Water Resources, Fish and Game,
Parks and Recreation, Forestry,
and Caltrans.
The Department of Conservation’s beverage recycling program
makes
$2 million in grant money available to nonprofit
organizations.
Under your tenure as Director of the Department,
the Department made quarterly awards from this money.
During the last award cycle before your resignation as
Director,
you approved the awarding of a grant to a nonprofit
organization called “Kids Can.”
Although the “Kids Can”
organization was notified that it had received the award,
you do
not believe that the process of awarding the money was completed.
Through an intermediary,
“Kids Can” is interested in hiring
your services to identify and assist in the application for grant
awards from other state, federal and private organizations.
“Kids
Can” clearly understands that you would not participate in
anything that has to do with the Department of Conservation or its
grant program.
ANALYSIS
The Act’s revolving door prohibitions which may affect your
work in your governmental relations firm are described in detail
in the advice letter to you dated October 18,
1995
(Byrne Advice
Letter,
No. A-95-337).
As discussed in that letter,
for a period
of one year, you may not represent any other person for
compensation before the Department of Conservation or any other
state administrative agency which you worked for or represented


File No.
A—95—384
Page
3
during the. 12 months before leaving office.
2
This prohibition
would preclude you from making any formal or informal appearance,
or any oral or written conununication, if the appearance or
communication is for the purpose of influencing the Department’s
administrative or legislative action or action involving
contracts.
(Section 87406.)
Under this provision,
you are
prohibited from making any contact with the Department of
Conservation and its staff to influence its award of any grant
moneys.
However,
Section 87406 does not prohibit you from working for’
Kids Can to identify and assist in applications for grant awards
from other state, federal,
or private organizations,
so long as
you do not attempt to influence the Department of Conservation’s
administrative or legislative action,
or action involving
contracts.
In addition,
you are permanently prohibited from representing
another person or aiding,
advising,
counseling, consulting or
assisting in representing another person before any court or state
administrative agency (or officer or employee thereof)
in any
judicial or other proceeding in which you participated as a state
administrative official.
(Sections 87400—87402.)
The permanent ban prohibits your involvement in any
proceeding, or specific matter between two parties,
in which you
participated while Director of the Department of Conservation.
You are prohibited from participating in such proceedings at the
Department of Conservation or any other state agency.
As Director
of the Department of Conservation,
you are deemed to have
“participated” in any proceeding which you supervised or which was
handled by the Department of Conservation staff during your
tenure.
(Brown Advice Letter,
No. A-91-033.)
Under this
prohibition, since you are deemed to have participated in the
grant of funds from the Department of Conservation to Kids Can,
you are permanently banned from any involvement in this grant.
application and award.
The permanent ban,
however, would not prohibit you from
identifying and assisting with grant applications for Kids Can
from other state, federal,
or private organizations, if these
applications are in no way related to the Department of
Conservation’s grant to Kids Can, because you would not be working
on the same proceeding in which you participated at the Department
of Conservation.
2
Any boards,
commissions or entities which the Department of
Conservation controls would be considered part of your state
administrative agency for purposes of this prohibition.
(Monagan
Advice Letter,
No.
A-93—473;
and Sybert Advice Letter,
No.
1—93-
380.)


File No.
A—95—384
Page
4
I trust this answers your question.
If you have any further.
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
916/322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
P. LL4—
By:
Hyla P.
Wagner
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC: HPW: ak

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you work for a nonprofit organization which received a
grant from the Department of Conservation during your tenure as
Director, if the work is unrelated to the Department of
Conservation or its grant program?

CONCLUSION: The Act’s revolving door provisions would not prohibit you
from working for a nonprofit organization which received a grant
from the Department of Conservation while you were Director,
as
long as
(a)
you do not represent the organization for compensation
before the Department of Conservation,
and
(b)
you do not
undertake work in any way related to the grant or any other
contract or proceeding in which the Department of Conservation and
the organization were involved while you were Director.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18995.

(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—384
